Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty chaotic, but I made it work somehow. I usually crammed my 15 hours of studying into whatever gaps I could find between work and other responsibilities. I'd often study late at night when things were quiet, printing out readings or copying notes into documents I could review anywhere. With six online courses, I had to be strategic about what got my attention each week.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was due and what fires I needed to put out in my life. I couldn't afford the luxury of a consistent schedule because my work hours changed week to week and I had limited resources to fall back on. Deadlines definitely dictated when I'd have intense study bursts versus weeks where I barely touched anything. I learned to accept that flexibility was my only option.
Q3: Usually it was checking if something was due soon or if grades had been posted. I'd also log in when I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while and the anxiety would start creeping up. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion or announcement that would prompt me to go in. It wasn't really a habit, more like responding to triggers.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS because my life was too unpredictable. The closest I got was checking in on Sunday nights to plan my week, but even that fell apart when things got busy. I think having low resources made it hard to build consistent habits around technology I couldn't always access easily.
Q5: The reading materials and written content were most useful to me because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs, print them at the library, and mark them up with my own notes. Discussion posts were also helpful when peers explained concepts in different ways than the instructor. I appreciated anything I could take offline and work with on my own time.
Q6: I barely watched the videos, maybe 19 minutes total, because they required stable internet and uninterrupted time I didn't have. I also ignored a lot of the optional supplementary materials because I had to prioritize what would actually count toward my grade. Some resources just felt like busywork that wouldn't help me pass, so I skipped them without guilt.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to assignments or exams, or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. I also valued anything that could be done efficiently without requiring me to be online for long periods. If I could see the practical payoff, I'd engage with it; otherwise, my limited time went elsewhere.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford to indulge in. Required activities got done because they had to, even if I was exhausted or anxious about them. With optional stuff, I'd think about whether it would actually help my grade or understanding, and usually the answer was that my time was better spent on requirements. The pressure of having six courses made me ruthlessly practical.
Q9: Absolutely, most of my studying happened offline. I'd download everything I could, print readings at the library, and study from my own handwritten notes. I used free textbook PDFs I found online and borrowed physical books when I could. The LMS was really just a portal to get materials, not where I actually learned.
Q10: There were many nights I spent hours reviewing printed notes and working through practice problems on paper that would show zero activity online. I'd also study with a classmate in person, quizzing each other and discussing concepts, which obviously didn't register anywhere. My best learning happened away from screens, in quiet corners of the library or at my kitchen table.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a lecture PDF or reading once, download it, and never open it in the LMS again. I'd work from that downloaded file for weeks, annotating it and reviewing it multiple times. The LMS would show I accessed it once, but I probably engaged with that material a dozen times offline.
Q12: My anxiety would spike during midterms and finals, making it hard to focus or even log in without feeling overwhelmed. There were weeks when I felt completely bored and disconnected from the material, like I was just going through motions. Financial stress and work demands also pulled my attention away from coursework during critical times. The CBT sessions helped me manage some of this, but it was still a struggle.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS felt like just another demand I couldn't meet. Confusion about course expectations made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to face what I didn't understand. Having six courses meant I was constantly triaging, and sometimes this course lost out to more urgent deadlines elsewhere.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials or click on readings without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or just too tired to process information. I'd technically be 'active' in the system but completely checked out mentally. Those sessions felt like wasted time, but I didn't know how else to push through.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts a lot because they felt low-stakes compared to assignments, even though I knew participation mattered. I'd also avoid starting big assignments until the last minute because the anxiety of beginning was worse than the anxiety of the deadline. Sometimes avoidance was just self-protection when I felt too overwhelmed to face another task.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to engage sometimes. I'd have bursts of activity right before something was due, cramming in reading and submissions. Without deadlines, I'd drift and let things slide, so they were actually helpful for keeping me on track. My whole semester was structured around due dates rather than any internal motivation.
Q17: When I got feedback, it either motivated me to try harder or made me want to avoid that type of assignment. Positive feedback on my assignment scores was encouraging and made me feel like my strategies were working. But if feedback was vague or critical without guidance, I'd feel more anxious and less likely to engage with similar tasks.
Q18: The structure of having everything online gave me flexibility I needed, but it also made it easy to disengage. Weekly modules helped me know what to focus on, but with my low attendance, I often fell behind the intended pace. I appreciated when the course had clear expectations, but sometimes the structure felt rigid when my life was anything but.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement much easier for someone in my situation. What made it harder was when things required synchronous participation or constant internet access. The discussion requirements felt forced sometimes, and the video-heavy content didn't match my learning style at all. I wish more courses recognized that not everyone can engage the same way.
Q20: I think those students might be anxious or confused, clicking around trying to figure things out without actually being able to complete work. They could also be dealing with external pressures that prevent them from following through on submissions. Sometimes high activity is just panic browsing, not productive engagement. I've definitely had moments like that myself.
Q21: That absolutely reflects my experience. My exam score and assignment scores were decent even though my LMS activity was low because I did most of my learning offline. I'm proof that engagement doesn't have to happen in a visible, trackable way to be effective. Some of us just learn better outside the system but can still demonstrate understanding when it counts.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns of when students access things and what they click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistent habits. But they completely miss offline studying, the quality of engagement during online time, and all the emotional and contextual factors affecting a student. They're like seeing shadows on a wall and thinking you understand the whole room.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actually thinking about the material and connecting it to things I already know or want to learn. It's when I care enough to put in effort even when it's hard, and when I feel like the work matters somehow. For me, engagement isn't about being online or visible; it's about whether my brain is actually working on understanding something.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I relied on printed materials and offline study to get through this course. It wouldn't capture the anxiety I managed through CBT sessions or how financial stress affected my ability to engage consistently. It also wouldn't show that despite everything working against me, I climbed my way to passing through sheer determination and strategic effort.
